frameworks.name: Causal Functional Connectivity (CFC)
frameworks.one_line: Directed, causal interactions between brain regions inferred from fMRI time series.

frameworks.name: Time-aware PC (TPC) algorithm
frameworks.one_line: Causal-discovery method used to infer directed CFC from resting-state fMRI.

frameworks.name: Correlation-based Functional Connectivity (sparse partial correlation)
frameworks.one_line: Conventional undirected FC baseline contrasted with the directed CFC view.

frameworks.name: Network-Based Statistics (directed)
frameworks.one_line: Network-level inferential framework used to localise altered directed sub-networks.

core_question: Which directed (causal) resting-state brain sub-networks are disrupted in Alzheimer's disease compared to cognitively normal individuals?
key_assumptions: Directional, causal interactions can be inferred from resting-state fMRI
Alterations in connectivity precede clinical AD symptoms
Network-level statistics are more informative than single-edge tests
manipulations: Group contrast: AD vs cognitively normal (CN)
Connectivity method: TPC-derived CFC vs sparse partial correlation FC
key_findings: Reduced directed CFC strength in AD involving inferior frontal gyrus, superior and middle temporal gyri, and cerebellum
Network-level (sub-network) alterations detected rather than isolated edge differences
interpretations: AD involves systemic, network-wide disruption of directed brain connectivity
Directed CFC offers diagnostic insight beyond conventional correlation-based FC
